Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I'm not gonna pretend I'm some expert on vaccines or healthcare policy. I work as a cleaner, and honestly, most of my day is spent making sure offices and homes are spotless, not diving into medical journals. But I've definitely noticed that vaccine conversations have become absolutely toxic on social media, and I think there's a way to handle them that doesn't involve everyone screaming at each other. From what I've seen scrolling through my feeds for hours every day, people are just talking past each other because nobody's actually listening anymore. When someone brings up vaccines around me, I try to remember that they probably have reasons for their perspective, even if I don't agree with them. My dad has a master's degree and he's always reading research papers, while my mom takes a different approach to health altogether, so I grew up seeing how educated people can still have different takes on things. That's actually been helpful in understanding why these conversations get so heated. I think the key is not pretending you know everything when you don't. I don't, and I'm cool admitting that. Instead of arguing about studies I haven't read or claiming I understand immunology when I clearly don't, I just ask questions and listen to what people actually think matters to them. Sometimes it's about control, sometimes it's about distrust in institutions, sometimes it's genuine concern. Those are all real feelings worth acknowledging. On social media, where I spend way too much time honestly, I've learned to just scroll past the arguments that are clearly designed to make people mad. It's not worth my energy. When someone I actually know brings it up, I'm straightforward but not aggressive about it. I respect that people have made different choices, and I expect the same back. It's not about winning a debate for me, it's about not letting this stuff destroy relationships that actually matter.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 22-year-old cleaner with moderate openness, low conscientiousness, low extraversion, and notably low agreeableness (20/100). This combination suggests someone who is somewhat flexible in thinking but not detail-oriented, prefers solitude, and is less focused on harmony in relationships. She spends several hours daily on social media, indicating exposure to polarized discourse. Her mixed family background (one parent with master's degree, one with no formal education) suggests exposure to diverse perspectives. Her low agreeableness and moderate openness indicate she won't shy away from disagreement but also won't be preachy. Her low conscientiousness suggests she won't be overly concerned with being perfectly informed before speaking. The tone should be casual, honest about limitations, somewhat dismissive of unnecessary debate, and grounded in personal observation rather than expertise. She would avoid pretending to know more than she does while also not being deferential or overly agreeable.
response_parsed.tone: Casual, honest, self-aware about limitations, slightly dismissive of performative arguments, pragmatic, introverted perspective on social discourse
citations: personal observation from social media engagement
